title: Flame Test
question: Why do different metal ions make different flame colors?
objective: Identify a metal sample by observing and recording its flame color
summary: Heating a metal ion excites its electrons. As the electrons return to lower energy levels, they emit characteristic wavelengths of visible light.

steps.title: Prepare the flame
steps.prompt: Begin by making the burner ready before introducing a sample.

actions.label: Light the Bunsen burner
actions.description: Open the simulated gas control and ignite a steady flame.

actions.label: Record a result now
actions.description: Write down a flame color before heating or observing any sample.

steps.title: Choose a sample
steps.prompt: Select one metal-ion sample and place it in the simulated flame.

actions.label: Test sodium
actions.description: Introduce the sodium sample to the burner flame.

actions.label: Test copper
actions.description: Introduce the copper sample to the burner flame.

actions.label: Test potassium
actions.description: Introduce the potassium sample to the burner flame.

steps.title: Observe the flame
steps.prompt: Look closely at the flame and compare its new color with the untested flame.

actions.label: Observe the color
actions.description: Pause and describe the hue produced by the selected metal ion.

actions.label: Skip the observation
actions.description: Remove the sample without looking at the flame color.

steps.title: Record the result
steps.prompt: Choose the record that treats your direct observation as evidence.

actions.label: Record the observed color
actions.description: Write the sample name and the exact flame color you just observed.

actions.label: Record a guessed color
actions.description: Ignore the observation and copy a color from memory.

outcomes.title: Science needs observation first
outcomes.message: There is no flame evidence to record yet; a careful observation must come first.
outcomes.explanation: Scientific records are useful when they describe evidence that was actually observed under known conditions.

outcomes.title: Orange-yellow flame
outcomes.message: The flame shifts to sodium's intense orange-yellow color.
outcomes.explanation: Excited sodium ions emit especially strong yellow-orange light near 589 nanometers.

outcomes.title: Green-blue flame
outcomes.message: The flame shifts to a vivid green-blue color from the copper sample.
outcomes.explanation: Copper compounds can emit blue-green light as excited electrons release specific packets of energy.

outcomes.title: Lilac flame
outcomes.message: The flame shifts to potassium's pale lilac color.
outcomes.explanation: Potassium's electron transitions include visible violet wavelengths, producing a lilac flame.

outcomes.title: Evidence missed
outcomes.message: The sample was removed before its flame color was observed.
outcomes.explanation: A result cannot support a conclusion unless the relevant change is observed and described.

outcomes.title: Spectral clue recorded
outcomes.message: Your record links a metal sample to the flame color you directly observed.
outcomes.explanation: Metal ions color flames because their electrons release element-specific energies as light when returning to lower energy levels.
